1: Ve-shavthi ani va-er'eh et-kol-ha'ashukot asher na'asim tahat hashemesh — 'I turned and considered all the acts of oppression done under the sun.' The verb shav (to turn/return) marks a new investigation — the Teacher redirects his attention from the times-poem meditation to social observation. Ashukot (H6217, acts of oppression) from ashaq (to oppress/defraud/extort). Ve-hineh dim'at ha'ashukot ve-ein lahem menachem — 'I saw the tears of the oppressed — they had no one to comfort them.' The pathos here is as acute as anywhere in the book: menachem (H5162, comforter) — the absent comforter. Lamentations 1:2 uses the same construction for Jerusalem's weeping with no comforter (ein menachem). The Teacher's observation anticipates Lamentations' grief without offering the resolution that comes only in the eschatological consolation (Isa 40:1: 'Comfort, comfort my people').
2: Ve-shabach ani et-ha-metim she-kebar metu — 'So I counted the dead who have already died more fortunate than the living.' Shabach (H7623, to praise/commend/declare fortunate) — the Teacher 'praises' the dead. The word is strong: this is public declaration of the dead's superior condition given the oppression of v.1. Min-ha-chayyim asher hemmah chayyim adenat — 'more fortunate than the living who are still alive.' The comparison is conditional on the context of oppression: death ends the experience of watching oppression without remedy. This is not a death-wish but an honest response to what the Teacher has observed.
3: Ve-tov mishneihem et asher-adenat lo hayah — 'But better off than either is the one not yet born.' The better-off formula tov … mi (good/better … than) creates a descending scale: living (worst), dead (better), unborn (best). Asher lo-ra'ah et-hama'aseh hara' sheassoh tahat hashemesh — 'who has not seen the evil that is done under the sun.' Not seeing the evil is the unborn's advantage. The Teacher's bitter logic: the best position is not to have begun. This is the nadir of his social observation — not a permanent conclusion but the honest extreme of what oppression without remedy looks like.
4: Ve-ra'iti ani et-kol amal ve-et kol kishron hama'aseh ki hi qin'at ish mere'ehu — 'Then I saw that all toil and every skilled achievement springs from a person's envy of his neighbor.' The word qin'ah (H7068, envy/jealousy/zeal) here in its negative sense: social rivalry as the hidden motor of industriousness. Kishron (H3788, skill/efficiency) appeared at 2:21 for the wise person's abilities. The Teacher now identifies that much of what appears to be virtuous skill (kishron) is actually rivalry-driven (qin'ah). Gam-zeh havel ure'ut ruach — the full verdict: industry driven by envy produces the same futile result as the other experiments.
5: Ha-kesil chofek et-yadav ve-okhal et-besaro — 'The fool folds his hands and devours himself.' The proverbial image of the fool who folds his hands (refuses to work) and thereby 'eats his own flesh' (consumes himself through idleness) is a counterpoint to v.4. The danger of v.4's observation (all toil is envy-driven) is the conclusion that one should do nothing; v.5 addresses that: idleness destroys too. The fool's hands, folded instead of working, turn inward and consume the person. The Teacher acknowledges the other side of the futility: not only is envy-driven labor futile, but its absence is self-destructive.
6: Tov melo kaph nachath mi-melo chafnayyim amal ure'ut ruach — 'Better is one hand full of quietness than two hands full of toil and chasing the wind.' The tov min (better than) comparative proverb is a wisdom form used frequently in Proverbs and Ecclesiastes. Nachath (H5183, quietness/rest/contentment) — the posture of the person who has enough and rests. The comparison is between one hand of contentment and two hands of toil-with-futility: not that toil is worse than idleness, but that toil driven by acquisitive anxiety (amal ure'ut ruach) is worse than a simpler, quieter sufficiency. This is the Teacher's moderate counsel between the envy-driven overwork of v.4 and the self-consuming idleness of v.5.
7: Ve-shavthi ani va-er'eh hevel tahat hashemesh — 'Again I turned and observed something futile under the sun.' The formulaic transition marks a new case study within the observation of futility. The repeated shav (turning) and ra'ah (seeing) signal the Teacher's methodological move to a new empirical case. This verse is transitional — its function is structural, introducing the case of the solitary miser in v.8.
8: Yesh echad ve-ein sheni gam ben ve-ach ein-lo ve-ein kets le-khol amalo — 'Here is a solitary man with no companion — neither son nor brother — yet his labor has no end.' The portrait of isolated, purposeless accumulation: echad (one/alone) with neither son (ben) nor brother (ach) to inherit. Ve-einenu sava' ashir — 'and his eyes are never satisfied with wealth.' Sava (to be satisfied/full) — the same root as the eye's insatiation (1:8) and the sea that is never full (1:7): the accumulator joins the relentless cycles of insatiability. U-le-mi ani amel ve-mechasar et-nafshi mi-tovah — 'He never asks, “For whom am I working, and why am I denying myself any pleasure?”' Mechasar nafshi (depriving/denying my soul/life) — the irony: the miser deprives the very nefesh that the Teacher elsewhere counsels should 'see good' (2:24). Gam-zeh hevel ve-inyan ra' hu — the formula closes with inyan ra' (evil/unhappy business): the same judgment as 1:13 on the whole investigating project is now applied to purposeless accumulation.
9: Tovim ha-shenayim min-ha-echad — 'Two are better than one.' The tov min comparative form now applies to social solidarity: the companionship that the solitary miser of v.8 lacks. Asher yesh lahem sakar tov ba-amalam — 'because they get a better return for their labor.' Sakar tov (good reward/return) — here, unusually, labor has a positive return when shared. The companionship proverbs (vv.9-12) do not reverse the verdict on amal generally but identify an exception: collaborative work produces returns that solitary accumulation does not.
10: Ki im-yipolu ha-echad yaqim et-chavero — 'For if one falls, the other will lift his companion up.' Chavero (his companion/friend) — from chaver (H2270, companion/associate). The image is both literal (helping someone who has fallen physically) and figurative (one who helps another recover from misfortune). Ve-oy lo le-echad sheyipol ve-ein sheni lahaqimo — 'But terrible is the fate of the one who is alone when he falls — there is no one to help him up.' Ve-oy lo — 'woe to him' — the lament exclamation marks the severity. The contrast is vivid: the two-person situation has a solution (one lifts the other); the solitary situation has no solution.
11: Gam im-yishkvu shenayim ve-cham lahem — 'Also, if two lie together they keep each other warm.' The practical wisdom is about survival in the cold — the ancient world before central heating required shared body warmth. Le-echad eikh yitcham — 'But how can one person keep warm alone?' The rhetorical question expects the answer: he cannot. The physical observation grounds the social teaching: community is not sentimental but functional — a matter of survival. This is the wisdom register of the companionship sayings, not romantic idealism.
12: Ve-im-yitqfoh ha-echad ha-shenayim ya'amdu negdo — 'Though one person may be overpowered, two can stand their ground.' Taqaf (H8630, to overpower/prevail over) — the single person is vulnerable to overpowering; the two can resist. Ve-ha-chut ha-meshulash lo be-mehera yinataq — 'A cord of three strands is not quickly broken.' Ha-chut ha-meshulash (the three-stranded cord) — a proverb within the passage, adding a third element to the two-is-better-than-one observation. The three-strand cord is structurally stronger than a two-strand one because the strands brace each other at more angles. The proverb is not introducing a theological third party (though Christian readers have applied it to divine presence in marriage/community); it is extending the numerical observation: two is better than one, and three is more stable than two.
13: Tov yeled misken ve-chakham me-melekh zaken u-kesil asher lo-yada lizhhar od — 'Better a poor but wise young man than an old and foolish king who no longer heeds counsel.' The tov min comparison returns: wisdom is a greater social resource than royal position when the king has ceased to be teachable. Lo-yada lizhhar od — 'who no longer heeds counsel' — literally 'who no longer knows to be warned.' Zahir (H2094, to warn/admonish/instruct) — the king who has stopped receiving instruction from others. The refusal of counsel is the signature of Proverbs' foolish king (Prov 12:15: 'the way of a fool is right in his own eyes'; 15:5: 'a fool despises his father's instruction').
14: Ki mi-beit ha-asurim yatza limlokh — 'The young man rose from prison to the throne.' The passive movement from prison to throne — without active verb of ambition — suggests the Teacher is describing a type or historical case. Ki gam be-malkuto nolad rash — 'though he had been born poor in that very kingdom.' The origin of the wise young man is the most disadvantaged possible: prison and poverty. His rise despite these origins is the evidence that wisdom transcends status. The most natural historical referent is Joseph (Gen 39-41: imprisoned, then elevated to rule), though the Teacher does not name him; the pattern fits other cases as well (Daniel, Mordecai).
15: Ra'iti et-kol-ha-chayyim ha-mehalkhim tahat hashemesh im ha-yeled ha-sheni — 'I saw all the living — those who walk under the sun — rally to the side of the second young man.' Ha-yeled ha-sheni (the second young man) — the 'second' (successor) who rallies popular support. The popular identification with the new young ruler is the moment of maximum promise for the young wise man who rose from prison. But v.16 will immediately undercut this popular support: it is transient.
16: Ein kets le-khol ha-am le-khol asher hayah lifneihem — 'There was no end to all the people who followed him.' The limitless crowd — a picture of popular success at its peak. Gam ha-acharonim lo yismechu vo — 'yet those who come later will take no delight in him either.' The succession problem of chs. 2-3 recurs: the generation that follows will not find the same satisfaction in the successor that the contemporary generation found. Popular acclaim has the same horizon as every other form of amal: it does not outlast the generation that gave it. Ki gam-zeh hevel ure'ut ruach — the full verdict formula closes this social meditation. The pattern is sealed: from the isolated miser to the beloved king, all social realities of accumulation and acclaim fall under the same judgment.
